# Capability Routing Standard

## Summary

Use the wiki, skills, and plugins together.

The wiki chooses the route, source order, and quality bar. Skills and plugins handle specialized execution when they materially improve the work. No layer should block a stronger applicable capability.

## Fast Decision

Before substantial work, make this small decision:

1. Is there a clearly applicable repo-local instruction or doc?
2. Is there a clearly applicable session-visible skill, plugin, plugin skill, app, or automation?
3. Which wiki page sets the quality bar or routing rule?
4. What is the smallest useful context packet to proceed?

If a skill or plugin clearly matches the task, use it. If the wiki clearly names a route, use it. If both apply, use the skill or plugin for execution and the wiki for constraints, verification, and source order.

## Default Pairing

- repo-local docs define project truth
- wiki pages define reusable standards and routing
- skills provide specialized workflows, tools, scripts, and execution details
- plugins provide bundled skills, apps, tools, and platform-specific workflows
- automations provide future or recurring follow-up when the user asks for monitoring, reminders, scheduled work, or continuation
- official docs resolve version-sensitive or platform-sensitive facts

## Context Budget Rule

Keep routing cheap:

- use the [[Task Routing]] read-depth tiers: Tier 0 route, Tier 1 lead page, Tier 2 conditional support, Tier 3 evidence
- read `Task Routing` or this page first when the route is unclear
- read the `SKILL.md` only for the chosen skill, not every nearby skill
- inspect plugin instructions or tool metadata only for the chosen plugin capability, not every installed plugin
- use the smallest useful set of wiki pages
- do not load the whole skill catalog unless the task is actually a skill-routing audit
- when no installed/session-visible skill or plugin fits, use fallback discovery only if the task would benefit from a specialist capability
- treat `Skill Routing Catalog` as a curated map, not an exhaustive list of every skill that may exist

## When To Prefer A Skill Or Plugin

- the user explicitly names it
- the task is specialized and a skill or plugin clearly owns the workflow
- it provides scripts, templates, checklists, source integrations, live tools, or platform-specific rules
- it is official, maintained, plugin-provided, or stronger than a generic wiki summary
- using it reduces guessing or repeated manual setup

## When To Prefer The Wiki

- the task is about route selection, source order, standards, or quality judgment
- repo-local truth needs to be combined with cross-project preferences
- multiple skills, plugins, or apps overlap and the agent needs a chooser
- the task does not need a specialist workflow
- using a skill or plugin would add more context than value

## When To Use Automation

Use an automation only when future execution is part of the request:

- remind, monitor, check later, keep watching, or follow up
- recurring reports or scheduled review
- heartbeat-style continuation in the current thread
- cron-style recurring work in a workspace

Do not create an automation just because a task is important. Use it when timing or recurrence is required.

## Reporting Rule

For substantial work, briefly report:

- wiki pages used
- skill, plugin, app, or automation used, if any
- why this was the smallest useful route

Example:

`Using: Capability Routing Standard, Skill Routing Catalog; skill: ui-polish-pass; reason: implemented UI exists and needs a focused polish pass.`
